replies.text: The bed of nails is actually quite comfy; and if you know physics (pressure over area) you know it's harmless; it's why a woman in heels will do more damage to a wooden floor than an elephant on said floor!

replies.author: @syndrome5372
replies.text: ​​​@4fgrrsen87l12dshas long as you make sure your body is spread out  and in contact with as many nails as possible, it's not different to just laying on them. Breeze blocks break with a sledge real easy if you hit them on the right side. And that's assuming they're using real blocks, which they're probably not. The boards they break are fake and designed to break too. And the block cushions the impact of the hammer. I work with stone and sometimes you need to smack them with a hammer to position them, but with thinner stones and copings you will break them if you hit them with a hammer, so you grab a block of wood, hold it on the stone and hit the wood with the hammer and it cushions the impact enough to not break the stone. Same principle.

replies.author: @ImXs1p3r
replies.text: @4fgrrsen87l12dsh the impact force of the sledgehammer is dissipated into shattering cinder blocks, only some reaches the body where it is arrives distributed over the bottom surface area of the brick. This force again distributes itself over the  body, which pushes down not one spot but over the entire body.

replies.author: @Fyrefrye
replies.text:
then the real "final" version of this challenge would be having the thickest/densest possible semicircular arc of candles and the longest sword the individual can handle. Set some method to light the candles, and then one single swing of the long sword overtop of the arc of candles would put them all out. This becomes a challenge in a few ways:
1. Controlling a very long sword and keeping it actually level during the swing. Long enough blades may even flex downwards when extended horizontally which could lead to hitting candles or missing wicks at some point in the swing. 
2. Candles lose height as they burn, so there's an inherent time limit to lighting them all before the initial candles lit shrink too much and drop out of the "plane of the swing." With a few candles, this is not an issue, but if you had to light hundreds or thousands? That might actually take a while.

author: @andrewphillips8341
text: The Original  GUINNESS book of records was published by the Guinness (beer) company.  They gave the books to pubs for free (after they bought extra beer). It was a great ad campaign for the company.  The original gimmick was that the book could solve augments  in pubs in a friendly way (usually with a bet . . . of beer).

replies.author: @robo5013
replies.text: Yes, the whole point of the 'world records' was that they were about silly things. One drunk in a pub claimed he could do a handstand for five minutes, or something, and people called BS so he did it, then someone else said I can do it longer, etc. It's all supposed to be for goofy fun.

replies.author: @drsch

Nah, it's still a more advanced juggling skill. It's not something that just anyone could do. It takes a lot of practice to be able to juggle pins/swords/whatever that require to you land on one specific side of the object. What he did is common amongst skilled jugglers, it's just that the record is stupidly specific about the type of sword he's juggling. Any street performer will juggle knives. This is a variation on it. 

I totally understand why he's juggling cheap mall ninja crap because I imagine he dropped those things at least 100 times learning to do that. No one wants to damage a several hundred dollar sword practicing.

author: @khujagrim
text: Guinness World Records was originally just a collection of world records for easy access, but with the advent of the internet people had an easier and more convenient way to look for world records. Other organization adjudicate all the legitimate records, so in order to justify their own existence Guinness started adjudicating stupid random records. Guinness doesn't measure the 100 metre dash but they will measure the 100 metre dash while wear clogs. Holding a Guinness World Record is a vanity project for people with nothing else in their life to give them joy.

author: @LicoMire
text: Friendly reminder: Bamboo is grass NOT Wood.

replies.author: @johannderjager4146
replies.text: In crafts and construction, it's considered wood, so put a semantic sock in it, Mr. "Um acthuallee".

replies.author: @LicoMire
replies.text: @johannderjager4146 in crafts it's actually called "bamboo wood", because botanically it's a grass.

replies.author: @OreoBambino
replies.text: This type of grass is wood! Not "a wood" but it consists of wood... Wood is defined as a natural composite of cellulosic fibers that are embedded in a matrix of lignin, found as xylem in the stems and roots of plants like trees, bushes, and other woody plants... So, is bamboo composed of cellulose and lignin? Yes, bamboo is composed of cellulose, along with other components like hemicellulose and lignin. Does bamboo have xylem? Yes, bamboo has xylem, which is a type of vascular tissue responsible for transporting water and nutrients throughout the plant. The xylem in bamboo is found within scattered vascular bundles in the stem, unlike in many trees where xylem is more continuously organized. Is bamboo a woody plant? Yes, bamboo is a plant. It is a type of woody perennial flowering plant that belongs to the grass family, Poaceae.

author: @karshthegoblin4179
text:
Regarding the Juggling Swords - As a retired circus performer I am annoyed that I lost my ownly guinus recort years ago, where I could have done this one without any effrot.
The weight only needs to be similar to juggling clubs, and the sword like objects would have their rotational weight in a similar location. This can be adjusted by adding weight to either end of the object.
In this case they would be more akin to juggling knives (or knife like objects in this case). Which can chop a banana, but can then be stopped by a top hat's felt.

author: @Kaeldorn
text:
The same fellow with the mats has a record for fastest tennis ball cut by a sword. That's one you might be able to challenge him on and it's definitely a better skill to measure.
Maybe a fun alternative challenge could be to set up a tennis ball machine that launches X number of balls in Y amount of time at distance Z and see how many you manage to cut with a sword.
Because it's automated and you're able to adjust settings for relatively consistent ball delivery, it then becomes about consistent strike accuracy on a moving target rather than a fluke of one in a hundred tries.

author: @spots_knight
text:
Guinness adds some conditions of their own to many records that are submitted to them. They do that so that any future attempts have specific guidelines that have to be followed in order to break an existing world record. 

For example, I'm not sure if you noticed that for the tatami matts, the cuts HAD to be in the 15 CM sections that were marked with the rubber bands. So it is possible that some of the cuts he made didn't count because they were outside of the 15 CM sections (It looked like a few were to my untrained eye) So he could not have just made more cuts per mat to get get a higher number.

author: @theWheezle
text:
I remember a video from TwoSetViolin a couple years ago looking at the Guinness record "fastest violin player". A lot of the same critiques. It's not actually impressive, it's a useless skill and achievement to track, and people that know absolutely nothing about that world or industry think it's impressive. 
Guinness has been a bullshit organization for decades. They will show up and officiate any record you want them to, if you pay them their substantial fee. Most of the stupid records like these are uncontested.

author: @jaykay6222
text: How about this. A challenge where you have to get a high count of tatami mat cuts, but you only have X tatami mats to work with. So to optimize the score you'd have to practice cutting off as little as possible. If that's not enough, also put a timer on it that is long enough that the max number of tatami mats comes into play, but short enough that you are still racing the clock. It'd be a test of accuracy but also endurance, being able to keep up a certain pace. Having watched the rest of the video, I see this was kinda mentioned already.

author: @euchale
text: The way the guiness world record works is that you pay them and say you want a record, and then they tailor a new world record specifically for you. Since no one else did it before, you automatically become the record holder. Thats why there are sometimes "most x eaten in 1 minute" and "most x eaten in 2 minutes" records, two different times means its 2 different records.

author: @jonathaneggers2907
text:
A friend of mine in Seattle, he cut off the wicks of twenty candles. They were all exact in height and such. The idea was to not topple any of them as they were free standing. Funny part is he only had a long sword so no cool katana. After twenty candles the wax of the wick start to stick to the sword. When we put out 22 (two rows on ten then to elven) every time those last two would catch on the wick just enough to topple. He was part of my D&D group and we did odd things when taking breaks. Loved doing Kendo with him. Lost a lot but won a few :). Man, 20 years ago doesn’t seem so long as I’m typing this. Any ways, perhaps a Katana that is proper sharp, not our long sword that just went over a wet stone for a minute or three could do better :).
Worst part of this was having to whittle down candles to get more wick, but we were a group of seven people so it was not too terrible.
